text: The heart rate deflection point (Conconi test) is not an accurate predictor of lactate threshold during running.
All subjects demonstrated a heart rate deflection when tested on a 400-m track with short stages, but only half showed a deflection on a treadmill with the same speed increments.
Shortening of stage durations at higher speeds on a track contributes to a loss of linearity in the speed-heart rate relationship, producing an artificial deflection point.
The heart rate deflection point overestimates lactate threshold when compared against a continuous treadmill protocol with 3-minute stages.
Differences in test protocols (track vs treadmill, stage length) explain discrepancies in whether a heart rate deflection point is observed during incremental exercise.
Lactate threshold should be determined using protocols with sufficiently long stages (e.g., 3 minutes) rather than short-stage Conconi-style protocols for accurate results.
